cards.text: To declare a constant variable in C++, use the {{c1::const}} keyword before the type.
A const variable {{c1::must be initialized}} when it is declared.
Syntax to declare a constant: {{c1::const int x{ 5 };}}
Const variables are also called {{c1::named constants}} or {{c2::symbolic constants}}.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Const variables must be initialized when declared. Also, you cannot assign to a const variable after declaration.
cards.explanation: The first line doesn't initialize x, and the second line attempts to modify a const variable, which is not allowed.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot assign to a const variable. Once initialized, const variables cannot be changed.
cards.explanation: The const keyword makes the variable read-only after initialization.

cards.front: What happens if you try to change the value of a const variable after initialization?
cards.back: The compiler will generate an error. Const variables are read-only after initialization.

cards.front: What are three benefits of using const variables instead of magic numbers?
cards.back:
• Makes code more readable and self-documenting
  • Easier to update values in one place
  • Prevents accidental modification of values

cards.explanation:
Use const variables when you have a value that:
  • Should not change during program execution
  • Needs a meaningful name (not a magic number)
  • Might need to be updated in the future (easier to change in one place)

cards.back: Keyword that makes a variable read-only after initialization
